section.title: Hosea's Marriage and Children
section.sectionRationale: The book opens not with a sermon but with a marriage. God tells Hosea to take an unfaithful wife and to name their children with names of judgment, turning the prophet's own household into a living picture of the LORD and faithless Israel. The chapter moves from three names of doom to a stunning promise of reversal.
section.thesis: God enacts His message through Hosea's family: a wayward wife pictures wayward Israel, three ominous names announce scattering, no mercy, and no covenant, yet the chapter ends with those very sentences overturned by grace.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the superscription; the command to marry Gomer and name Jezreel; the births of Lo-ruhamah and Lo-ammi; and the sudden promise that reverses every name.

verses.text: This is the word of the LORD that came to Hosea son of Beeri in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah, and of Jeroboam son of Jehoash, king of Israel.

When the LORD first spoke through Hosea, He told him, “Go, take a prostitute as your wife and have children of adultery, because this land is flagrantly prostituting itself by departing from the LORD.”
So Hosea went and married Gomer daughter of Diblaim, and she conceived and bore him a son.
Then the LORD said to Hosea, “Name him {{jezreel|Jezreel}}, for soon I will bring the bloodshed of Jezreel upon the house of Jehu, and I will put an end to the kingdom of Israel.
And on that day I will break the bow of Israel in the Valley of Jezreel.”

Gomer again conceived and gave birth to a daughter, and the LORD said to Hosea, “Name her {{loRuhamah|Lo-ruhamah}}, for I will no longer have {{racham|compassion}} on the house of Israel, that I should ever forgive them.
Yet I will have compassion on the house of Judah, and I will save them, not by bow or sword or war, not by horses and cavalry, but by the LORD their God.”
After she had weaned Lo-ruhamah, Gomer conceived and gave birth to a son.
And the LORD said, “Name him {{loAmmi|Lo-ammi}}, for you are not My people, and I am not your God.”

units.label: The promise that reverses the names

verses.text: Yet the number of the Israelites will be like the sand of the sea, which cannot be measured or counted. And it will happen that in the very place where it was said to them, ‘You are not My people,’ they will be called ‘sons of the living God.’
Then the people of Judah and of Israel will be gathered together, and they will appoint for themselves one leader, and will go up out of the land. For great will be the day of Jezreel.

jezreel.gloss: Jezreel means God sows. It was the valley where Jehu's dynasty shed blood to seize the throne, so the name first sounds like judgment on that house. Yet the same word can mean God plants a people in the land, and by verse 11 it turns into a word of hope, the great day of Jezreel.

loRuhamah.gloss: Lo-ruhamah means she has not received mercy, or not pitied. The name announces that God will withhold His tender compassion from faithless Israel. It is built on the root racham, the deep, motherly mercy that God says He will no longer show, until He reverses it in chapter 2.

loAmmi.gloss: Lo-ammi means not My people. It is the undoing of the covenant formula, I will be your God and you will be My people. To name a child this is to declare the covenant bond severed, the most devastating word Israel could hear, and the one the gospel most gloriously reverses.

racham.gloss: To have compassion, to show tender mercy. The word is related to the Hebrew for womb, picturing the visceral, protective love of a parent. Its removal in the name Lo-ruhamah is the withdrawal of that mercy; its return in chapter 2 is the heart of God's restoring grace.

basic.context: Hosea preached to the northern kingdom of Israel in its final decades before Assyria destroyed it. God gave him a shocking assignment: marry an unfaithful woman and let his family become a sermon. Each child receives a name that spells out the coming judgment, scattering, no mercy, and no longer My people. Then, without warning, God promises to turn every one of those names around.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a true account of a prophet's life used as a sign. People debate how much of the marriage is literal, but the meaning is plain: Israel has been unfaithful to God like a spouse who strays, and judgment is coming. Yet the last two verses are pure promise, and the New Testament quotes them as fulfilled when God brings in a people from all nations.
basic.originalLanguages: The three names carry the message. Jezreel means God sows, first a place of bloodshed, later a word of planting and hope. Lo-ruhamah means not pitied, built on racham, the tender, womb-like mercy of God. Lo-ammi means not My people, the reversal of the covenant promise. In verse 10 all of this flips: they will be called sons of the living God.
basic.typology: Hosea acting out God's message with his own family points to the God who does not merely speak His love but enacts it, ultimately in the incarnation. The reversal of not My people into My people is the pattern by which God brings outsiders into His family through Christ.
basic.christ: Paul quotes verse 10 in Romans 9 to explain how God calls a people from among the Gentiles, and Peter echoes it in 1 Peter 2:10, once you were not a people, but now you are the people of God. The one leader Israel and Judah gather under, verse 11, points to the Messiah, great David's greater Son, under whom the scattered are gathered.
basic.god: We meet a God who feels His people's betrayal as a wounded husband, who will not wink at sin, yet whose final word is not rejection but restoration. His mercy outlasts His judgment; He announces the reversal before the judgment has even fallen.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 9:25-26 and 1 Peter 2:10 both quote this chapter to describe the church. 2 Kings 10 records the bloodshed of Jezreel. Exodus 6:7 gives the covenant formula that these names undo and then restore.
basic.oneStory: The chapter sets the whole book in motion: a holy God bound in covenant love to an unfaithful people, judgment that is real, and mercy that is greater still. The promise that not My people will become My people runs straight to Christ, who gathers a countless family, Jew and Gentile, under one Head.

context.literary: The superscription dates the book, then a terse narrative of marriage and three births drives the message home through names rather than speeches. The unit closes with an abrupt turn to promise in verses 10 and 11, a pattern of judgment folding into hope that will recur throughout Hosea.
context.historical: Hosea ministered in the eighth century BC to the northern kingdom, Ephraim or Israel, during its last, unstable years before the Assyrian conquest of 722 BC. Jeroboam II's reign was prosperous but spiritually corrupt, and the house of Jehu, founded in the bloodbath at Jezreel, was near its end.
context.canonical: Hosea stands first among the twelve Minor Prophets. Its opening chapter supplies the New Testament with key language for the inclusion of the nations, and it establishes the marriage metaphor that runs through Scripture to the marriage of the Lamb in Revelation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce, through the prophet's own marriage and children, that covenant Israel has betrayed the LORD and faces judgment, while planting the promise that God will yet make a vast people who are called His own.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The command to Hosea is a unique prophetic sign, not a pattern believers are to imitate. What abides is the truth it pictures: the seriousness of covenant unfaithfulness and the greater seriousness of God's restoring mercy.

debates.question: Was Gomer already a prostitute when Hosea married her, or did she become unfaithful later?

views.label: A prostitute from the start
views.case: Verse 2 plainly says take a prostitute as your wife, so the sign is set from the wedding day: God knowingly binds Himself to an unfaithful partner.

views.label: Faithful at first, unfaithful later
views.case: Some read the phrase proleptically, describing what Gomer would become, so the marriage mirrors Israel, which began in covenant faithfulness and then strayed.

debates.bearing: Either way the picture holds: the LORD enters covenant with a people He knows will betray Him, and His love is not naive but clear-eyed and costly.

study.originalLanguages: The sermon is preached in three names. Jezreel, God sows, is first the valley of Jehu's bloodshed, a name of judgment, but the same root will bloom into promise, the great day of Jezreel when God plants His people. Lo-ruhamah is not pitied, from racham, the tender mercy tied to the word for womb; God says that mercy is withdrawn. Lo-ammi, not My people, tears up the covenant formula of Exodus 6:7. Then verse 10 overturns everything: not My people become sons of the living God, and the countless offspring recall the promise to Abraham.

typology.type: Hosea enacting God's message with his own family
typology.fulfillment: God who does not only speak but embodies His love, finally in the Son who takes to Himself an unfaithful people.

typology.type: Lo-ammi, not My people, reversed to My people
typology.fulfillment: The gathering of Jews and Gentiles into one people of God in Christ, Romans 9:25-26 and 1 Peter 2:10.

typology.type: The one leader under whom Israel and Judah are gathered
typology.fulfillment: The Messiah, the Son of David, who unites and leads the scattered flock, John 10:16.

study.christ: The chapter's closing promise is explicitly claimed by the apostles for Christ's church. Paul cites verses 10 and part of 23 of the next chapter in Romans 9:25-26 to show that God is now calling a people who were not His people, including the Gentiles. Peter takes the same words in 1 Peter 2:10. The one leader of verse 11, under whom a divided people is gathered and brought up out of the land, points to Jesus, the good Shepherd who has other sheep to bring into one flock. In Him every name of judgment is answered: the scattered are sown, the unpitied receive mercy, and outsiders are called sons of the living God.
study.god: The LORD here is a jilted husband and a grieved Father, not a distant lawgiver. He takes Israel's idolatry personally, as adultery, and His judgment is just: mercy withheld, covenant severed. Yet the same God speaks restoration before the axe has fallen, promising a people beyond counting and a mercy that will outrun His wrath. He is holy enough to judge and gracious enough to save the very people He judges.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Hosea to show God calling a people, including Gentiles, who were not His people.
Once you were not a people, but now you are the people of God; once you had not received mercy, but now you have.
The covenant formula, I will be your God and you will be My people, that the names Lo-ammi undoes and God restores.
The bloodshed of Jezreel by the house of Jehu, which the name of Hosea's son recalls.

study.oneStory: Hosea 1 lays down the book's great tension: a faithful God bound to a faithless people. The names spell judgment, yet the last word is grace, a countless people called sons of the living God under one gathered leader. That promise reaches its fulfillment in Christ, who makes not My people into My people and gathers the scattered into one family forever.

quiz.q: Why did God tell Hosea to marry an unfaithful woman?

options.text: To make his marriage a living picture of the LORD's covenant with unfaithful Israel.
options.note: Verse 2 ties the marriage directly to a land prostituting itself by departing from the LORD.

options.text: To teach Hosea patience through personal hardship.
options.note: There was real cost to Hosea, but the stated purpose is the prophetic sign, not personal growth.

options.text: Because no faithful woman was available to the prophet.
options.note: The text gives no such reason; the marriage is a deliberate sign from God.

options.text: To show that God approves of unfaithfulness.
options.note: The opposite is true; the marriage exposes the horror of unfaithfulness to God.

quiz.q: What do the three children's names announce?

options.text: Coming judgment: bloodshed at Jezreel, no more mercy, and no longer My people.
options.note: Jezreel, Lo-ruhamah, and Lo-ammi each spell out an aspect of judgment on Israel.

options.text: Only future blessing and prosperity for Israel.
options.note: Blessing comes at the end, but the names themselves are names of judgment.

options.text: The names of Israel's future kings.
options.note: They are sign-names about the nation's covenant standing, not royal names.

options.text: That God had forgotten His people entirely.
options.note: The chapter ends by promising restoration, so God has not forgotten them.

quiz.q: How does the chapter reverse its own names of judgment?

options.text: It promises that not My people will be called sons of the living God, and the scattered will be gathered.
options.note: Verses 10 and 11 overturn Lo-ammi and Jezreel into hope.

options.text: It says the judgment was only a misunderstanding.
options.note: The judgment is real; grace overturns it rather than cancelling that it was deserved.

options.text: It removes all consequences immediately.
options.note: The reversal is a future promise; the judgment still falls in history.

options.text: It ends with no hope at all.
options.note: The chapter ends on a strong note of hope and restoration.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament use Hosea 1:10?

options.text: Paul and Peter apply it to God calling a people, including Gentiles, into His family in Christ.
options.note: Romans 9:25-26 and 1 Peter 2:10 cite Hosea to describe the church.

options.text: It is treated only as a prediction about the modern state of Israel.
options.note: It concerns the people of God gathered in Christ, which the apostles apply to the church.

options.text: It is never referenced in the New Testament.
options.note: It is quoted directly by both Paul and Peter.

options.text: It is used to prove the covenant is permanently cancelled.
options.note: It is used to show mercy and inclusion, the very opposite of cancellation.

quiz.q: What do we learn about God from this opening chapter?

options.text: He feels betrayal like a wounded husband, judges sin truly, yet promises mercy greater than His wrath.
options.note: The chapter holds together God's just judgment and His restoring grace.

options.text: He is indifferent to whether His people are faithful.
options.note: He is far from indifferent; unfaithfulness grieves Him deeply.

options.text: He is only angry and offers no hope.
options.note: Real anger is present, but the chapter ends in hope and restoration.

options.text: He changes His mind because He was mistaken.
options.note: His mercy is not a correction of error but the outworking of His steadfast love.

questions: God turned Hosea's whole household into a message. Where might God be asking you to let your ordinary life, not just your words, speak of Him?
Israel's idolatry is described as adultery against a faithful God. What lesser loves compete for the devotion you owe to the LORD?
The name Lo-ammi, not My people, is the most painful word in the chapter, yet it is the one grace most fully reverses. How does it move you that God turns outsiders into sons of the living God?
God announced restoration before the judgment even fell. How does knowing that His mercy runs ahead of His discipline shape the way you come back to Him after failure?
Peter says, once you had not received mercy, but now you have. How would living today in the light of that received mercy change the way you treat others?

translationNotes.note: Jezreel means God sows. The name first recalls the bloodshed by which Jehu's house took the throne, then is turned toward hope in verse 11.
Lo-ruhamah means she has not received mercy, or not pitied, from the root racham, tender compassion related to the word for womb.
Where the BSB prints an em dash before not by bow, it has been rendered with a comma per house style.
Lo-ammi means not My people. A Hebrew reading of the final clause is I am not yours, sharpening the severing of the covenant bond.
Cited in Romans 9:26. The promise of offspring like the sand of the sea deliberately echoes the covenant with Abraham in Genesis 22:17.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hosea 1:1-11. Em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas per house style.
